coredata.dc:title: An ultra-high-sensitivity electrochemiluminescence aptasensor for Pb2+ detection based on the synergistic signal-amplification strategy of quencher abscission and G-quadruplex generation
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xiaohong
Li, Libo
Li, Fang
Zhao, Wanlin
Luo, Lijun
Bi, Xiaoya
Li, Xia
You, Tianyan
coredata.dc:description: Signal amplification provides an effective way to improve detection performance. Herein, an ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence (ECL) aptasensor for Pb2+ detection was developed based on a dual signal-amplification strategy of the abscission of a quencher and the generation of a G-quadruplex by one-step and simultaneous way. Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots linked with complementary DNA (cDNA-NCQDs) at the sensing interface was applied as the quencher of a tris(4,4′-dicarboxylic acid-2,2′-bipyridyl)ruthenium(II) (Ru(dcbpy)3 2+)/tripropylamine system to minimize the ECL signal due to the intermolecular hydrogen bond-induced energy-transfer process. Upon the addition of Pb2+, its specific binding with the aptamer triggered the abscission of cDNA-NCQDs, accompanied by the formation of G-quadruplex on the surface of the electrode, both of which amplified the intensity of the light emission. The ECL amplification efficiency induced by the above two mechanisms (78.6%) was valuably greater than that of their sum value (69.3%). This synergistic effect resulted in high detection sensitivity of the ECL aptasensor, which allowed to thereby obtain Pb2+ measurements in the range of 1 fM - 10 nM with an ultra-low detection limit of 0.19 fM. The Pb2+-mediated synergistic signal-amplification ECL strategy can provide a new approach for integrating various amplification strategies.

(A) EIS and (B) ECL responses of different modified electrodes: a: GCE, b: AuNPs/GCE, c: Apt/AuNPs/GCE, d: MCH/Apt/AuNPs/GCE, e: cDNA-NCQDs/MCH/Apt/AuNPs/GCE and f: Pb2+/cDNA-NCQDs/MCH/Apt/AuNPs/GCE. EIS curves were conducted in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution containing 0.1 M KCl.

(C) ECL responses of the as-developed ECL aptasensor incubated with different interfering metal ions. (D) Repeatability (intra- and inter-assays) of the proposed ECL aptasensor. head.ce:title: An ultra-high-sensitivity electrochemiluminescence aptasensor for Pb2+ detection based on the synergistic signal-amplification strategy of quencher abscission and G-quadruplex generation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Signal amplification provides an effective way to improve detection performance. Herein, an ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence (ECL) aptasensor for Pb2+ detection was developed based on a dual signal-amplification strategy of the abscission of a quencher and the generation of a G-quadruplex by one-step and simultaneous way. Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots linked with complementary DNA (cDNA-NCQDs) at the sensing interface was applied as the quencher of a tris(4,4′-dicarboxylic acid-2,2′-bipyridyl)ruthenium(II) (Ru(dcbpy)3 2+)/tripropylamine system to minimize the ECL signal due to the intermolecular hydrogen bond-induced energy-transfer process. Upon the addition of Pb2+, its specific binding with the aptamer triggered the abscission of cDNA-NCQDs, accompanied by the formation of G-quadruplex on the surface of the electrode, both of which amplified the intensity of the light emission. The ECL amplification efficiency induced by the above two mechanisms (78.6%) was valuably greater than that of their sum value (69.3%). This synergistic effect resulted in high detection sensitivity of the ECL aptasensor, which allowed to thereby obtain Pb2+ measurements in the range of 1 fM - 10 nM with an ultra-low detection limit of 0.19 fM. The Pb2+-mediated synergistic signal-amplification ECL strategy can provide a new approach for integrating various amplification strategies.

ce:section.ce:para: Soil contamination with different heavy metal ions has posed an alarming health threat globally because they can enter the agricultural ecosystem and transfer to the human body through the food chain (Ashraf et al., 2020, 2017; Ravichandiran et al., 2020, 2021a, 2021d). Among most hazardous heavy metal ions, Pb2+ not only inhibits the growth and disturbs the normal metabolism of plants, but also seriously affects crop yield and grain qualities, and ultimately endangers human health because of its non-degradable and toxic features (Ashraf et al., 2017; Meng et al., 2020). Especially, Pb2+ can induce irreversible nerve injury and behavioural dysfunction, which results in a serious threat to children (Li et al., 2020). Resultantly, the determination of trace amounts of Pb2+ in soil samples is vitally significant from a health perspective. Several types of spectrometry arrays for Pb2+ mainly comprise atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) (Barbosa et al., 2016), flame AAS (FAAS) (Kamel et al., 2019), capillary electrophoresis-inductively coupled plasma‐mass spectrometry (CE-ICP-MS) (Li et al., 2017), and inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) (Ebrahimi-Najafabadi et al., 2019). Despite the high accuracy and good selectivity, these spectrometry techniques are still limited by sophisticated instruments and professional operators (Ravichandiran et al., 2021b, 2021c). Alternatively, comparable high sensitivity and simple operation endow electrochemistry (EC) (Guo et al., 2016; Ma et al., 2020), fluorescence (FL) (Li et al., 2020), photoelectrochemistry (PEC) (Meng et al., 2020) and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) (Ni et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019a; Zhang et al., 2020a, 2020b, 2020c) methods with superior sensing value for Pb2+ analysis.

ECL aptasensors that integrate the high specificity of aptamer and exceedingly good sensitivity of the ECL method exhibit good analytical performance (Wang et al., 2019a, 2020, 2021; Hesari and Ding, 2016; Zhang et al., 2020a, 2020b, 2020c; Sun et al., 2018). The improvement of the detection sensitivity of ECL aptasensors is the keystone to meet the requirements for ultra-trace detection of Pb2+ (Fang et al., 2020). The amplification of the ECL signal is prominently contributing to the increase of the detection sensitivity. Generally, signal probe-labelled sandwich methods are used as one of the most common amplification strategies to achieve high-sensitivity detection of antibodies or nucleotides (Anusha et al., 2019; Dong et al., 2020). However, owing to the small-size characteristic of Pb2+, it is very difficult to establish a reaction with the capture probe and the labelled probe simultaneously, which limits the development of detection strategies for Pb2+. To solve such problems, the target-mediated cyclic amplification technologies based on the nicking-nuclease digestion of a Pb2+-aptamer complex have been employed as effective methods for the detection of Pb2+ (Peng et al., 2018), but often are affected by enzyme inactivation and false reactions (Zhao et al., 2021). Fortunately, the recent research that the formation of G-quadruplex at the sensing interface could amplify the ECL signal of tris(2,2′-bipyridyl) dichlororuthenium(II) (Ru(bpy)3 2+)-based complex/tripropylamine (TPA) pair provided a new method for the highly sensitive detection of Pb2+ accompanied by simplifying the experimental steps (Wang et al., 2019a).

Additionally, to explore the high-sensitivity detection of Pb2+, diverse ECL aptasensors with different signal output modes, such as “on-off” (Peng et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2021), “off-on” (Hai et al., 2014; Wei et al., 2014) and “on-off-on” (Sun et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019a, 2021), have been established by linking the interacted probes with cDNA. An off-on output mode typically offers a higher signal-to-noise ratio than an on-off mode because of the enhanced ECL signal (Shao et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2016). In most “on-off-on” ECL aptasensors, Ru(bpy)3 2+-based reagent (Ru(II))/TPA systems have been utilized to realize the first “on” due to its strong background signal and outstanding ECL efficiency. Combined with Ru(II)/TPA systems, some well-developed quenchers, such as ferrocene (Cao et al., 2006), methylene blue (Lu et al., 2020b), pristine multiwall carbon nanotube (Tang et al., 2013), graphene oxide (GO) (Huang et al., 2015, 2020) and graphitic carbon nitride nanofibers (Peng et al., 2018), have positively been explored to minimize the background signal to complete the signal establishment of the “off” state. In the presence of the target, the abscission of the quencher-linked cDNA is triggered, and the recovery of ECL signal is successfully obtained so that the sensing mode is switched from “off” to “on” state (Hai et al., 2014; Sun et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019a). Of note, inspired by the above-pointed carbon-based nanomaterial quenchers, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), featuring good water-solubility, superior stability, small size, and good biocompatibility, have gathered wide attention (Luo et al., 2019; Zhai et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2018), and some of them also show superior quenching features in the Ru(II)/TPA ECL systems (Liu et al., 2021b; Yang et al., 2020). Similarly, our previous research reported that nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs) could act as a novel type of quencher for the tris(4,4′-dicarboxylic acid-2,2′-bipyridyl)ruthenium(II) (Ru(dcbpy)3 2+)/TPA system, and the quenching mechanism involved an energy-transfer process from the excited-state Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ to the intermolecular hydrogen bonds (Liu et al., 2021b).

Therefore, combining the quenching feature of NCQDs and the amplified function of G-quadruplex, a highly sensitive ECL aptasensor was constructed for Pb2+ detection with a dual signal-amplification principle. The working mechanism of the ECL aptasensor was depicted in Scheme 1. NCQDs linked with cDNA (cDNA-NCQDs) was immobilized on the electrode surface through the hybridization between aptamer and cDNA to obtain the minimized ECL signal of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA solution system via the interaction of intermolecular hydrogen bonds between NCQDs and Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ (Liu et al., 2021b). Upon the addition of Pb2+, cDNA-NCQDs was released from the sensing interface, and Pb2+-induced G-quadruplex was generated simultaneously on the electrode surface, resulting in the successful ECL signal recovering. The synergistic effect produced by the two amplification strategies could lead to excellent sensitivity. The developed ECL aptasensor based on the synergistic signal-amplification strategy realized the high-sensitivity ECL analysis of Pb2+.

Tris(4,4′-dicarboxylicacid-2,2′-bipyridyl) ruthenium(II) dichloride (Ru(dcbpy)3Cl2, 98.0%, Suna Tech Inc), tripropylamine (TPA, ≥98.0%, Sigma-Aldrich), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98.0%, TCI), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98.0%, TCI), Tetrachloroauric (III) acid trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O, ≥ 99.9%, Acros Organics), 6-mercapto-1-hexanol (MCH, 98.0%, Macklin), Graphene oxide (GO, Xianfeng Nano), and Tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris, 99.8–100.1%, Aladdin) were employed. Potassium ferricyanide (K3Fe(CN)6), potassium ferrocyanide (K4Fe(CN)6), sodium dihydrogen phosphate dihydrate (NaH2PO4·2H2O), disodium phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O), lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2, ≥ 99.7%), hydrochloric acid (HCl), nitric acid (HNO3), citric acid, urea, ethanol and acetone were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). The ultrapure water provided by a microporous system was applied for the preparation of all solutions.
The used aptamer and cDNA were provided by Sangon Biotech (China), and the sequences were as follows:
Aptamer (Apt): 5′-SH-(CH2)6-GGT TGG TGT GGT TGG-3′.
cDNA: 5′-NH2-AAC CAC ACC AA-3′.
Aptamer1 (Apt1): 5′-GGT TGG TGT GGT TGG-3′.
cDNA1: 5′-SH-(CH2)6-TTT TTT AAC CAC ACC AA-3′.
Aptamer2 (Apt2): 5′-NH2-GGT TGG TGT GGT TGG-3′.
cDNA2: 5′-AAC CAC ACC AA-3′.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was obtained by TECNAI F20 (USA). A Thermo ESCALAB 250 instrument with Al Kα radiation (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used to record the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) and FL spectra were measured on a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and FS5 FL spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK), respectively. The Zeta potentials were measured on Malvern Nano-ZS Zeta Sizer (Malvern, UK). Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was carried out with Nicolet iS10 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The EC and ECL experiments were carried out on an MPI-EII ECL analyzer (Xi'an Remax, China) by a three-electrode system with a glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm) as working electrode, a platinum wire as counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was evaluated by Autolab PGSTAT 302N EC workstation (Metrohm, Netherlands).

NCQDs was synthesized by a one-step microwave-assisted method according to the previous report (Qu et al., 2012). For the preparation of cDNA-NCQDs, 800 μL the mixed solution containing 10 mM EDC and 5 mM NHS was added into 1600 μL 5 mg mL−1 NCQDs, and further stirred for 15 min to activate the -COOH groups on NCQDs surface. Subsequently, 1600 μL 1 μM amino-functionalized cDNA was added, and cDNA-NCQDs was successfully prepared after stirring the above mixture for 2 h. cDNA-CQDs and cDNA-GO were similarly prepared via the above process, but replaced NCQDs with CQDs and GO, respectively.
A 20% polyacrylamide gel was prepped with 1 × TAE buffer and was run at 120 V for 100 min. The gel was stained with 3 × Super GelBlue™ for 30 min. Then, it was photographed in a gel imaging analyzer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the ECL aptasensor and ECL measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, a layer of AuNPs was obtained by immersing clean GCE into 1% HAuCl4 solution under the electrodeposition condition at − 0.2 V for 20 s (Liu et al., 2021a). Next, 6 μL 0.2 μM thiol functionalized Pb2+ aptamer, whose binding constant with Pb2+ has been reported as 1.5 × 106 M−1 (Smirnov and Shafer, 2002), was incubated on the AuNPs/GCE surface by Au-S bonds for 12 h. Then, 1 mM MCH was applied for 1 h to cover the non-specific adsorption sites. Next, 6 μL cDNA-NCQDs was dropped on the above electrode surface which was placed in an incubator to react at 37 °C for 2 h. Lastly, the obtained modified electrode was immersed in 100 μL Pb2+ solution for 40 min to capture Pb2+. After each modification, the acquired electrode was thoroughly rinsed with the corresponding washing buffer.

The above-obtained modified electrode was put into the phosphate buffer (PBS) solution containing 0.16 mM Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and 10 μM TPA. The change in the value of the ECL signal allowed the Pb2+ detection. The scanning potential range was set from 0.2 to 1.25 V with the scanning speed of 0.1 V s−1, and the photomultiplier high voltage of 700 V was applied.

Tap water, river water, farmland soil, soil near chemical factory, and contaminated soil samples were all collected from Jiangsu province. Both tap water and river water samples were treated according to the reported methods (Li et al., 2021; Peng et al., 2018). The water samples were firstly centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and then the supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 µm cellulose membrane for further use. Farmland soil, soil near chemical factory, and contaminated soil samples were treated according to previously reported criteria (HJ 803-2016) with minor modification (Xie et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021a, 2021b). Specifically, the soil samples were air-dried and finely ground to a 200-mesh nylon sieve. The mixture solution of 0.1 g soil sample and 6 mL nitric acid/hydrochloride (HCl/HNO3, v/v, 3/1) was gradually added into a conical bottle to carry out heat digestion on an electric heating plate. After digestion, a 0.22 µm cellulose membrane was used to filter impurities, and 1 M NaOH was used to adjust the pH value of the extraction solution to 7.0. Finally, some of the obtained solution was directly detected by ICP-OES method, and the others were further diluted by 10,000 times for analysis by the as-developed ECL aptasensor.

UV–Vis absorbance spectroscopy was carried out to test the optical properties of cDNA-NCQDs, demonstrating its successful preparation. A distinct absorption peak of the bases of the cDNA strand at 260 nm (curve a) is observed (Inset of Fig. 1A) (Zhu et al., 2020). Two well-defined absorbance peaks located at 336 and 405 nm for NCQDs (Other characterizations are shown in Fig. S1) are attributed to the π-π* conjugate structures of sp2 hybridised carbon (Fig. 1A, curve b) (Qu et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2021a). After the amide reaction between cDNA and NCQDs, the characteristic peak of cDNA shifts from 260 to 254 nm (Fig. 1A, curve c), revealing the formation of new chemical bonds (Li et al., 2021).

FL measurements were also conducted to confirm the optical properties of cDNA-NCQDs. As exhibited in Fig. 1B, the FL emission peak of NCQDs at 536 nm remains almost unchanged before and after the modification with cDNA, illustrating the stable FL properties of cDNA-NCQDs (Bi et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2013).

Zeta potentials were directly measured to evaluate the electrophoretic mobility of cDNA, NCQDs, and cDNA-NCQDs according to the Henry equation (Bi et al., 2021). Fig. 1C exhibits that the Zeta potentials of individual cDNA (curve a) and NCQDs (curve b) are − 18.4 and − 16.2 mV, respectively. However, the potential value of cDNA-NCQDs (curve c) is smaller (− 10.7 mV) than individuals, because the increased mass and size can decrease the electrophoretic mobility (Bhattacharjee, 2016; Jiang et al., 2009). This change of Zeta potentials indirectly proves the successful modification of NCQDs with cDNA (Bi et al., 2021).

FT-IR measurements were also applied to verify the conjugation of cDNA and NCQDs. As displayed in Fig. 1D, the characteristic peaks of cDNA (curve a) at 3407 and 1627 cm−1, corresponding respectively to the NH and CO bonds, are observed (Liu et al., 2013). The characteristic peaks of NCQDs (curve b) at 1713, 1602, and 1398 cm−1 are related to the CO, COO- and CN bonds (Li et al., 2015; Qu et al., 2012). The corresponding peaks of cDNA-NCQDs appear at 1710, 1627, and 1401 cm−1 (curve c), and the peak positions have a small shift compared with those of NCQDs, which further illustrates the successful modification (Liu et al., 2013).

XPS survey spectroscopy was collected to analyze the elemental composition of cDNA, NCQDs, and cDNA-NCQDs. Fig. 1E exhibits that four distinct peaks of cDNA at 286.6, 401.7, 532.8, and 133.2 eV corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s and P2p of adenine and cytosine are observed (curve a) (May et al., 2004). While the peaks of NCQDs appeared at 284.8, 399.8 and 531.5 eV are related to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively (curve b) (Li et al., 2015; Qu et al., 2012). After the amide reaction, cDNA-NCQDs displays all characteristic peaks of cDNA and NCQDs (curve c), and the P2p element should come from cDNA (Fig. 1F), likewise proving the successful formation of cDNA-NCQDs (Li et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations and feasibility of the ECL aptasensor for Pb2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The construction and target recognition process of the ECL aptasensor was performed by EIS ( Fig. 2A) that fitted with the Randles equivalent circuit (Inset of Fig. 2A) (Zhu et al., 2021). Compared with bare GCE (curve a, 203.6 Ω), the electron transfer resistance (Ret) value of AuNPs/GCE decreases to 38.7 Ω (curve b), illustrating the good electrical conductivity of AuNPs (Huang et al., 2021). Upon the modification of the aptamer, Ret increases to 42.9 Ω (curve c) since a negatively charged aptamer can hinder the diffusion of [Fe(CN)6]4-/3- (Wang et al., 2019a). When MCH is applied on Apt/AuNPs/GCE, a further increase of Ret is observed (387.9 Ω, curve d). With the addition of cDNA-NCQDs, Ret raises to 590.9 Ω (curve e) owing to the negative electricity of cDNA-NCQDs, indicating the formation of double-stranded DNA between the aptamer and the cDNA. After Pb2+ is captured, a reduced Ret (curve f, 460.1 Ω) is obtained since the peeling off of cDNA-NCQDs from the electrode surface can decline the hindrance of the diffusion of [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− (Wang et al., 2019a). The variation trend of EIS results is further verified by CV behaviours with the same path (Fig. S2). In addition, PAGE experiments demonstrate that Pb2+ has a much stronger binding ability with the aptamer than cDNA (Fig. S3), which reveals the successful recognition process of Pb2+ and its aptamer.

Subsequently, the feasibility of the as-developed aptasensor for Pb2+ detection was estimated by recording the ECL responses. Fig. 2B shows that the ECL signal of bare GCE attains to 7475 a.u. (curve a) in a PBS solution containing 0.16 mM Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and 10 μM TPA because of the high-efficiency ECL emitter/coreactant pair. When AuNPs is electrodeposited on the GCE surface, the ECL signal decreases to 5464 a.u. (curve b), which may be ascribed to the energy transfer from Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ to AuNPs (Lu et al., 2020a; Zeng et al., 2020). After modifying with DNA and MCH, the ECL signals decline to 2188 a.u. (curve c) and 1937 a.u. (curve d), respectively, owing to their poor conductivity. The ECL signal sharply decreases to 651 a.u. when cDNA-NCQDs is incubated on the electrode surface (curve e) because of the hydrogen-bond-induced quenching performance of NCQDs for Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ ECL signal (Liu et al., 2021b). Upon the addition of Pb2+, Pb2+ can compete with cDNA-NCQDs and specifically bind with its aptamer, leading to the generation of G-quadruplex on the electrode surface. Simultaneously, cDNA-NCQDs is separated from the sensing interface, thereby resulting in the blocking of the intermolecular hydrogen bonds between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs (Liu et al., 2021b). Furthermore, the energy transfer from Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ to the intermolecular hydrogen bonds is blocked, so that an unneglectable enhancement of the ECL signal is observed (curve f, 3044 a.u.). The results demonstrate the reasonable construction of an ECL aptasensor for Pb2+ detection using Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA as a luminous system and NCQDs as the quencher.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functioning mechanism of the ECL aptasensor for Pb2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To improve the superiority of the ECL aptasensor, two commonly quench-type carbon-based nanomaterials (CQDs and GO) were linked with the cDNA, and the quenching efficiency of NCQDs for the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA system was highlighted by using cDNA as background contrast. It can be observed from Fig. 3A that the quenching degree of cDNA-CQDs (26.7%) is smaller than that of cDNA-NCQDs (66.6%) since the absence of nitrogen elements on the CQDs surface decreases the number of hydrogen bonds between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and CQDs. GO can also show good quenching behaviour because of the oxygen-containing groups on its surface and its intrinsic properties. But its quenching degree (42.0%) is still smaller than that of NCQDs (66.6%), which may be ascribed to the smaller size and better water solubility of NCQDs (Huang et al., 2015, 2020; Liu et al., 2021a). Resultantly, the quenching-degree order of the three carbon-based nanomaterials on the electrode surface for Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA system is NCQDs > GO > CQDs, which is also verified by directly adding them into the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA solution system (Fig. S4). In the presence of Pb2+, the recovering efficiencies of ECL signals are proportional to the quenching degrees of the three different quenchers (Fig. 3B), following the order of NCQDs > GO > CQDs. Furthermore, we designed three ECL aptasensors (a, b, and c) based on the above quenchers (cDNA-NCQDs, cDNA-CQDs, and cDNA-GO) (Fig. S5A), and investigated their ECL signals at different concentrations of Pb2+. The results show that all the ECL signals of the aptasensors increase with the enhancement of Pb2+ concentrations from 0.1 pM to 1 nM (Fig. S5B). Under the same concentration range of Pb2+, the sensitivities of the three ECL aptasensors are 576.8, 293.3, and 451.4, respectively, demonstrating that cDNA-NCQDs-induced maximum signal amplification leads to the highest sensitivity. However, only the release of cDNA-NCQDs from the electrode surface can lead to such an excellent recovering performance, which is of notice.

It has been reported that the formation of G-quadruplex on the electrode surface can amplify the ECL signal (Wang et al., 2019a). To better understand the working mechanism of the as-developed ECL aptasensor, four sensing models defined as I, II, III, and IV were designed by adjusting the position of the G-quadruplex (Fig. 3C). In Model I, cDNA and aptamer were successively modified at the sensing interface, and the specific binding of Pb2+ with the aptamer triggered the release of Pb2+-G-quadruplex from the electrode surface. The results show that the ECL signal has almost no enhanced behaviour (Fig. S6A). Interestingly, when the modification order of cDNA and aptamer is exchanged (Model II), the G-quadruplex formed on the electrode surface upon the addition of Pb2+ enables to recover about 36.2% of the ECL signal (Fig. S6B), which illustrates that the formation of the G-quadruplex at the sensing surface indeed can be used as a signal-amplification strategy. To further prove the effect of the formation of the G-quadruplex on the ECL signals, NCQDs serving as the quencher were introduced into Model III and Model IV, respectively. In Model III, the ECL signal recovery is approximately 33.1% (Fig. S6C), which only comes from the release of cDNA-NCQDs. However, the ECL recovery attains to approximately 78.6% (Model IV, Fig. S6D) because of the simultaneous release of cDNA-NCQDs and generation of G-quadruplex. These results demonstrate that the release of cDNA-NCQDs and the generation of G-quadruplex on the electrode surface can both amplify the ECL signal. Notably, the ECL amplification induced by Model IV (78.6%) is valuably greater than the sum value of Model II and IV singly (69.3%). There is 9.3% the synergistic effect that is evaluated by calculating the difference value between model IV and model II+III (Fig. 3C).

To understand the mechanism behind the synergistic effect, two groups of control experiments were carried out by shielding the nitrogen element in NCQDs and the carboxyl groups in Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ respectively. As exhibited in Fig. S7, the synergistic effect of cDNA-CQDs-induced aptasensor (3.4%) is smaller than that of cDNA-NCQDs (9.3%) (Fig. S6), because the absence of nitrogen elements in the CQDs decreases the number of hydrogen bonds between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and CQDs. Additionally, when Ru(bpy)3 2+ is selected as the luminophore to shield the carboxyl groups of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+, there is almost no synergistic effect, which may be attributed to the situation that there is no intermolecular hydrogen-bond formation between Ru(bpy)3 2+ and NCQDs (Fig. S8). The above two experimental results show that the synergistic effect decreases with the decline of intermolecular hydrogen bonds, illustrating the synergistic effect indeed can promote the ultra-high sensitivity of the ECL aptasensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performances of the ECL aptasensor for Pb2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To further reveal the superiority of the synergistic effect, the analytical performances of the ECL aptasensors induced by different modes for Pb2+ detection were investigated under optimal conditions (for experimental parameters, see Fig. S9). As can be seen from Fig. S10A, the ECL signal obtained by model II gradually increases with increasing Pb2+ concentrations from 10 fM to 10 nM, and exhibits a great linear relationship with the logarithmic concentration of Pb2+ (LogCPb 2+) (Fig. S10B). The linear regression equation is IECL = 2605.0 + 266.1 LogCPb 2+, with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9951. As can be seen from the regression equation, the sensitivity of the ECL aptasensor is 266.1, and an estimated limit of detection of 4.41 fM is obtained according to the international union of pure and applied chemistry (IUPAC) definition (The calculation of the limit of detection, SM). Simultaneously, the model III-based ECL aptasensor shows a linear range of 10 fM - 10 nM, a sensitivity of 227.4, and an estimated limit of detection of 7.46 fM (Fig. S10C and D). As expected, compared with model II and III, the model IV-based ECL aptasensor exhibits the widest linear range of 1 fM - 10 nM, the highest sensitivity of 590.6, and the lowest limit of detection of 0.19 fM ( Fig. 4A and B, Table S1). The results illustrate that the novel synergistic effect induces an ultra-high sensitivity of the ECL aptasensor. More advantageously, the as-developed ECL aptasensor exhibits the superior analytical performance in comparison to some previously reported methods ( Table 1). The widest linear range and the lowest detection limit are acquired because the dual signal-amplification strategy produces a novel synergistic effect.

The selectivity of the ECL aptasensor was estimated by recording the signal variations in the presence of common interfering metal ions, including K+, Na+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, and Ni2+. As displayed in Fig. 4C, the ECL signal changes of the aptasensor caused by 10 nM interfering metal ions (the concentration is 1000-fold higher than that of Pb2+ concentration) and their mixture are within 10% (Columns 2–14) compared to the blank group (Columns 1), which should be attributed to the analysis errors. However, 10 pM Pb2+ and the mixture solution of all metal ions exhibit a recovered ECL signal (Columns 15 and 16). Therefore, such an ECL aptasensor allows the specific detection of Pb2+.

The repeatability evaluated by intra- and inter- assays was studied by collecting ECL intensity. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the ECL intensity of intra-assays of five aptasensors is 4.36% in the same batch, and that of inter-assays of five aptasensors is 4.37% in different batches (Fig. 4D), illustrating satisfactory repeatability. In addition, the stability of the developed ECL aptasensor for Pb2+ sensing is evaluated by monitoring the ECL signal for consecutive 30 cycles (Fig. 4E), and RSD is calculated to be 1.05%. After storing the ECL aptasensor at 4 °C for 7 days, its ECL signal remains at 90.6% of the initial signal (Fig. 4F). The above results demonstrate the good stability of the ECL aptasensor.

The practicability of the dual signal-amplification ECL aptasensor for Pb2+ determination was certified by the standard addition method in tap water, river water, farmland soil, soil near chemical factory, and contaminated soil samples. As exhibited in Table 2, the recoveries of the spiked tap water, river water, farmland soil, and soil near chemical factory are between 94.0% and 106% with RSDs below 6.23% (For the original data, see Fig. S11). The accuracy of the as-developed aptasensor was verified by ICP-OES method. The recoveries obtained by the ICP-OES method are between 96.2% and 110%.

Furthermore, contaminated soil sample was also investigated. Pb2+ in the sample is detected 1.83 μM by the as-developed ECL aptasensor (Table 2). Meanwhile, the concentration of Pb2+ obtained by ICP-OES method is 1.90 μM, which is consistent with that of the ECL method, illustrating the accuracy of the ECL aptasensor. The recoveries of the spiked contaminated soil fall in the range of 92.7–109% with RSD lower than 2.56% (Table 2; For the original data, see Fig. S11). Both methods exhibit good recoveries for Pb2+ in the contaminated soil sample. The results indicate that the as-developed ECL aptasensor possesses a great potential application for Pb2+ detection in real samples.

This study describes an ultrasensitive ECL aptasensor for Pb2+ determination based on the abscission of cDNA-NCQDs coupled with the simultaneous generation of G-quadruplex. cDNA-NCQDs was verified as an excellent quencher and fixed on the electrode surface by hybridization with aptamer to obtain a minimized signal of a Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA aqueous solution. In the presence of Pb2+, both the release of cDNA-NCQDs and the generation of G-quadruplex effectively amplified the recovering efficiency of the ECL signal. The detection ability of the ECL aptasensor based on this synergistic effect was enhanced by regulating the formation position of the G-quadruplex. The obtained ECL aptasensor had a wide range and an ultra-low detection limit of 0.19 fM with excellent selectivity, repeatability, and stability. In addition, the ECL aptasensor was successfully used to detect Pb2+ in water and soil samples, and the results were well consistent with those of ICP-OES. The synergistic signal-amplification strategy could broaden the application for the highly sensitive detection of Pb2+ in real-life samples.
